metadata.note: Uniform sharp-sphere model; point cross section, RMS conversion, experimental corrections, and proton-radius distinction corrected.

skin_warnings: B14: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Elastic electrons scattered from an extended charge distribution interfere. In a simple spherical model, the angular pattern can contain a first diffraction minimum that carries a size scale.

props.command: How can a diffraction minimum constrain a nucleus's charge radius?
props.segment: The First Dark Minimum and Nuclear Size
props.runningText: mapping scattering angle to momentum transfer…
output: The form factor is a Fourier-space charge profile.
A model maps its first zero to a radius scale.

beats.narration_text: The useful object is not a literal optical ring on a screen but a minimum in the differential count versus angle or momentum transfer. Experimental smearing may keep that minimum from reaching exactly zero.
What radius does the first minimum measure, and which assumptions turn its position into a number?
First correct a common shortcut: a point charge does not generally scatter isotropically. The point-particle Coulomb or Mott cross section already has strong angular dependence.

Under plane-wave Born-style factorization, the extended-target cross section is the point result times the magnitude squared of a charge form factor. The form factor is normalized to one at zero momentum transfer.

For a spherical charge density, the form factor is its radial Fourier transform. Small q probes broad structure; larger q resolves finer spatial variation, though accessible q and corrections limit the reconstruction.
For a uniform sharp sphere, F of q equals three times spherical Bessel j one of qR divided by qR. Its first zero occurs at qR about four-point-four-nine-three.
In that model, destructive interference among contributions from the entire volume makes the amplitude vanish at the first zero. It is not simply left half canceling right half pair by pair.

For elastic scattering with incident wave number k, q equals two k sine theta over two. Measure the first-minimum angle, compute q zero, then infer the model's sharp radius R equals four-point-four-nine-three divided by q zero.
That R is the edge radius of the uniform-sphere model. Its RMS charge radius is square root of three-fifths times R. A diffuse real nucleus needs a fitted density model, not one universal conversion.

Finite surface thickness, nonspherical structure, Coulomb distortion, relativistic corrections, inelastic contamination, detector resolution, and incomplete q coverage all affect the inferred density. Heavy nuclei especially require distorted-wave treatments.

Your turn. A uniform-sphere form factor has its first minimum at q zero equals zero-point-seven-five inverse femtometers. Estimate the sharp radius R using four-point-four-nine-three divided by q zero.
Four-point-four-nine-three divided by zero-point-seven-five is about five-point-nine-nine femtometers. The corresponding uniform-sphere RMS radius is about four-point-six-four femtometers.
The First Diffraction Minimum and Nuclear Size. Liam, in for Bear.
